text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 19 October 2015 

Dr Amelia Hart
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Hart
Consultant Neurologist,  

Jonathan Peters, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K728315 NHS No. 9076312548  
12 Oakfield Terrace, Dulwich, London SE21 8HT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient, whom I saw in the epilepsy clinic today. He attended with his partner and was able to give a detailed account of his events. He has a history consistent with focal epilepsy. 

He has practised aura recognition strategies previously and is able to describe his warning symptoms clearly in his own words. He reports a rising internal sensation “like a lift going up,” coupled with a metallic taste and a brief sense of detachment from surroundings; these typically last 10–20 seconds. On some occasions these auras progress to impaired awareness with behavioural arrest and right-hand fumbling, and he may utter brief, incomprehensible speech. Post-event confusion is short (under 5 minutes) and he usually retains partial recall of the aura itself. 

Regarding temporal pattern, he notes that most problematic spells tend to cluster around the time he is first getting up in the morning, particularly when he has slept poorly the night before. He describes this as “morning-on-rising” occurrences, with smaller auras on several weekdays and a more disruptive episode about once every 2–3 weeks. There have been no convulsions reported, and no clear nocturnal events. 

He keeps a diary and uses a phone prompt to rehearse his pre-agreed grounding techniques when the warning appears (slow breathing, sitting down, and alerting his partner). He feels these measures occasionally prevent escalation to impaired awareness. Known precipitating factors include sleep restriction and missed meals. There is no alcohol misuse. 

Examination today was unremarkable. Speech, cranial nerves and limb power were normal. No focal neurological deficits were detected. 

In summary, this is focal epilepsy with preserved and well-articulated auras, with progression to focal impaired awareness events intermittently, more commonly noted around getting up in the morning. We discussed optimising sleep hygiene and continuing the aura response plan he has been using. I have arranged an up-to-date EEG and will request a high-resolution MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) given the focal semiology. I will also review his current antiseizure medication regimen at our next visit once we have results to guide targeted adjustment. 

Safety advice was reinforced, including supervised bathing and avoidance of heights when auras occur. He understands when to seek urgent care. We will provide him with an updated seizure action plan reflecting his focal epilepsy and aura management techniques. 

I will see him again in three months or sooner if there is any deterioration. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you need further details. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes focal epilepsy with frequent auras and intermittent progression to focal impaired awareness. It states: 'smaller auras on several weekdays' and 'a more disruptive episode about once every 2–3 weeks.' We must choose the highest frequency across event types. 'Several weekdays' implies multiple auras on most weeks, but no exact numeric count is provided. Because the exact number per week is not stated, we cannot convert it to a precise per-week value. The other frequency given (once every 2–3 weeks) is lower frequency than the auras. Therefore, the highest frequency is the auras, but their exact frequency is unspecified. Per the rules, when seizures are mentioned but specific frequency cannot be determined, return 'unknown'.
